content.problemStatement:
Chef and Chefina are playing a game. There are NNN piles where ithi^{th}ith pile contains AiA_iAi​ stones.

The players take alternate turns with **Chef starting the game**. In his/her turn, a player can make **one** of the following type of move:


Type 111: Choose **any** non-empty pile and remove 111 stone from that pile.
Type 222: Remove 111 stone each from **all** the NNN piles. This move can be done only if **all** the NNN piles contain at least 111 stone.
The player who cannot make a move loses the game.

Determine the winner of the game if both players play optimally.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN denoting the number of piles.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,A3,…,ANA_1, A_2, A_3, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,A3​,…,AN​ denoting the number of stones in each pile initially.




Output Format
For each test case, output `CHEF` if Chef wins the game, `CHEFINA` otherwise.


You may print each character in uppercase or lowercase. For example, the strings `CHEF`, `chef`, `Chef`,  and `cHEf` are all considered the same.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 5⋅1055\cdot 10^55⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Chef can make a move of type 222 and remove 111 stone each from all the piles.
Thus, Chefina has no valid move to make and she loses.
Test case 222: Consider the following sequence of moves:

Chef removes 111 stone each from all piles. Thus the remaining stones are [0,1,2][0, 1, 2][0,1,2].
Chefina removes 111 stone from pile 222. Thus the remaining stones are [0,0,2][0, 0, 2][0,0,2].
Chef removes 111 stone from pile 333. Thus the remaining stones are [0,0,1][0, 0, 1][0,0,1].
Chefina removes 111 stone from pile 333. Thus the remaining stones are [0,0,0][0, 0, 0][0,0,0].

Thus, Chef has no valid move to make and he loses.
